sections.content: A Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) is a portable 406 MHz distress beacon designed to be carried by an individual. Unlike an EPIRB, which is registered to a vessel and typically float-free mounted, a PLB is registered to a person and manually activated. PLBs transmit to the same Cospas-Sarsat satellite system as EPIRBs.

PLBs are smaller and lighter than EPIRBs, making them suitable for personal carry on deck, in a liferaft, or even during shore-based activities. Most modern PLBs include an internal GPS for precise position reporting. Battery life is typically 24 hours minimum, compared to 48 hours for an EPIRB.

sections.title: Registration
sections.content: Like EPIRBs, PLBs must be registered with the national authority. Registration information includes the owner's name, emergency contacts, and a description of typical activities. Unregistered beacons may result in delayed SAR response.

sections.prompt: What is the main difference between a PLB and an EPIRB?

options.text: PLBs use a different satellite system
A PLB is registered to a person; an EPIRB is registered to a vessel
EPIRBs are manually activated; PLBs are automatic

sections.explanation: The key distinction is registration: a PLB is registered to a person and carried individually, while an EPIRB is registered to a vessel and typically mounted in a float-free bracket on the ship.
